speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three with Frances Flumenbaum. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you want to know now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next, after I came back to the camp, I still was in camp for months. The American people came. They already established sort of places for people to go. Certain friends who I knew very well, I survived with them, they were living already in the German houses. Because they asked the Germans to leave town just for a short while. And then they came back. But just as a punishment, the Russian people through them out from their own houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wasn't eager to move in nowhere. I was in camp, remaining in camp. One girlfriend came to me. She said to me, why are you sitting here? You can establish a life already.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can get rest. You even can have nice things in your possession. You just come along. But I wasn't ready. And I was in camp there for months till I met my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband happened to pass by that camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I met him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at that time, it's not [NON-ENGLISH]. But you fall in love, which I see now how important it is. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'm sure my husband fell in love with me, too. We got to know each other. He was with a group of people. And I was still in that camp. The room was clean. In the same bed we was sleeping [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till my husband said to me, let's get out from here. So we did, in groups, a lot of people together-- men and women. The men were helping us go through the borders. And I remember it wasn't easy to pass one border, but we did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we came to Munich. We wind up in Munch. In Munich, we got married. Thanks God, my husband survived with a sister. I adore her. She's a little bit older than my husband. And she was, right away, like a mother to me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we still are very, very close. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my sister, at that time when I told you that she went to Vienna to look with her friend for her brother, she couldn't come back. Because it was very hard to travel.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she stayed in Vienna for a while. But we knew that we alive. And we knew where we are. And we were in contact through The Red Cross. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in Munich, we got married. The name of the rabbi who married us was [INAUDIBLE]. What was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewer
text: What day did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1947, we got married. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had, at my wedding, my mother's uncle, through a second marriage [INAUDIBLE]. That uncle was, to me, with the feelings, like my own uncle. Some cousins-- my sister couldn't make it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A year later, God blessed us with a wonderful son. Only one son, but a wonderful son.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, in the United States, they call him Bill, William. At that time, Wolf, Abraham Wolf, carrying the names of my father and my husband's father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We stayed in Munich for a while, because we couldn't immigrate to the United States. One reason was that I-- my lungs wasn't in perfect condition health-wise. And at that time, the United States did not let in anybody who had certain-- I mean-- difficulties with health. They wanted to heal the people in Germany and come healthy to the United States. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we stayed in Munich. We raised our child till nine in Munich. And then we sent him to Switzerland to a strictly Hebrew, Orthodox home for children in [INAUDIBLE]. And the reason was I did not want my son to get the feeling that I had in Poland, that he is Jewish. And he should be growing up in a healthy atmosphere. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not that I could afford at that time what we paid, but we managed to do this. And my son was in Switzerland till 13. At 13, he was bar mitzvah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We chose the place for his bar mitzvah, a place where only a small town. And this was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember the name right now. And there was built a home for elderly people, a synagogue. And we choose this place-- it was more Jewish to me and surrounded by Jewish people, by the people that I-- I wanted and I liked to see. And an order not to have German music playing at my son's bar mitzvah. I have a cousin living in Paris. I wrote him a letter. And I called him up. And I asked him, I promise to pay as much they're going to ask me. Even though I cannot afford it right now, but I will pay off. If you can send me-- it's only a distance-- what they can make it here in five, six, eight hours. And he said to me, I'll do it for you. The name of the music-- of the leader of the music was [? Starsky. ?] And they drove overnight. And he had Jewish music, a Jewish atmosphere. And he had an American rabbi. He was in the army. I think in [INAUDIBLE]. And he came with his wife, with his children. And he was in a [INAUDIBLE]. And he had a beautiful bar mitzvah, even though it was in Germany, but with a different surrounding. Because we couldn't make it in time to the United States. And after the bar mitzvah, a few months later, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went to Israel with the mind maybe we will settle in Israel. But my sister was already in the United States. My uncle and my sister were in Toronto. My all cousins, mostly the family, which is the closest sisters, were in the United States. And the rest of the family who remained still in Israel, they told me, you should go where your sisters are, that your child should grow up with the closeness of family, having an aunt next to you, like you used to have it. So we settled in Los Angeles. It didn't work out exactly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess, destiny. This was our destiny, to live in Los Angeles. Our son, from the beginning, he went to school in Los Angeles in high school, and then to college in Berkeley. He made a mensch of himself with our help, I have to say. We contribute something. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He got married. And we have a wonderful daughter-in-law, wonderful two grandsons. And also, again, it was [NON-ENGLISH], the destiny, to have him in so close by in same city. And I do hope till the rest of lives. Because they used to live in New York. And we used to travel to New York three, four times a year for celebrations of holidays mostly and also birthdays. They used to come here to see us. But the distance seemed, when we got older, very far.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now, thanks God, they live close by. You going to see them later on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, again, in certain ways, I have to say, we are lucky. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But thinking back-- back to my youth, I wouldn't call myself lucky. I don't have reason to say I'm lucky. Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I survived? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Let me say, it's easier to live when you believe. God want it that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you think the Holocaust changed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In different ways. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Still, I'm thinking, why is that happen to people? Nobody deserved it, I wouldn't say, nobody. But like the Bible said, punishments from God came to people when they turned away from him, like Noah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Noah survived, because God wanted him. And at that time, he promised-- God promised himself not to do this anymore to people. So why did he do this now? So that question is not solved.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm not going to say what other people say, I don't believe in God anymore. Because I believe. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I wish my children would believe in God, also. They do believe. I'm sure of that. It changed me also to cherish, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to respect, not take life for granted. Every minute, to say, thanks, God, for the little favors. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And mostly and mainly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that people should not forget. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I have to say this to my son. He should never forget that 1942, two sets of grandparents were perished in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And not only the grandparents, he knows. He would have uncles and aunts.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what's happened to the rest? We are united. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And to think about, and not to forget, and to repeat from generation to generation-- and by repeating and not to forget, because history repeats itself, this will be one step towards preventing.

speaker: interviewee
text: It shouldn't happen again. What we do now, for instance, we do support the ADL. Because this is the organization would try to prevent a lot of anti-Semitic acts. Everybody knows about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on top of my line, to have our country, because Israel is there where our goal is in having a country and knowing the feeling of not having. And this we know. Our survivors know that, including me. And I want this our children to remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know what else really to tell you more. I'm sure I had more and more to go, but there's no end to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father. The picture was taken in 1941. They had already cut his-- part of his beard. And he didn't look like that. As you see, how frightened he looks. They needed that picture for a passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in front of our house.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture that I took after the war when I went to see the grave of my grandfather. And as you see, in Hebrew, it says his name-- [NON-ENGLISH] Hersch Blady. It says here, also, when he died and that he died with honesty. And it meant a lot, saying on a grave.

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture-- do you want me to start on the left or on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Left is my sister, Sarah. She was, at that time, eight years old. My little brother is six, Hersch. And my older brother, I think he was 10 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was during the war also.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941 at the same time when it was taken for my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the picture was taken, I think, at the end of 1938--

speaker: interviewee
text: --or the beginning of 1939, sometime during this time. In Poland, in Slawkow, also.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my identification card from 1945 as soon we arrived in Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me, Frances Flumenbaum, and my husband, now Sam Flumenbaum, 1947 after we got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me, Frances Flumenbaum, holding my darling little boy a few months old in 1948, in Munich, Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, he was Wolfgang Flumenbaum.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Sam Flumenbaum. God bless him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. From left. Next to him, it's me, Frances Flumenbaum. Next to me is my lovely daughter-in-law, [? Trisha ?] Flumenbaum, and my son, Bill Flumenbaum now in the United States. And my son is-- next to my son is my oldest grandson, [? Jonathan ?] Flumenbaum. And next to my daughter-in-law is my little one, [? Alex ?] Flumenbaum.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in few months ago in 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles in the UCLA. Here, was the bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the bar mitzvah of my son. It's taken 1961. To the left, next to the menorah, is my husband, Sam Flumenbaum. Next to him is our son, William Flumenbaum. Next to my son, it's me, Frances Flumenbaum. And next to me is the rabbi and his wife, the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, now I'm going to say this is my charming son, William Flumenbaum, the day of his bar mitzvah in temple with full of respect, beautiful inside and outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Frances, would you like to introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'm starting from the right side. This is my lovely daughter-in-law, [? Trisha ?] Flumenbaum, till 120. And this her older grandson, [? Jonathan ?] Flumenbaum.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my grandson, her oldest son. Sorry. And this is my son, William, next to me. On his lap is his youngest son, Alex. And all the way the left is my 120 [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: William, would like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but there's-- so much to be said, really. But what [? Trisha ?] said before you put the tape on-- that this is a whole family. And I think when you think about what my parents survived and what others did not, to have a whole family 50 years later is something that has greater meaning to me today Than it did as a young child or as an adolescent when this experience is really one the overwhelms you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would anybody else like to say anything? [? Jonathan? ?] [? JONATHAN FLUMENBAUM: ?] No. Go ahead. [? JONATHAN FLUMENBAUM: ?] Cheese.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's all right. That's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say anything to your grandma? [? ALEX FLUMENBAUM: ?] No.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to say something to me? Maybe you like I should give you something? What would you like to say to me?

speaker: interviewer
text: [? ALEX FLUMENBAUM: ?] May I have a cookie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, you could have a cookie. And what else? [? ALEX FLUMENBAUM: ?] May I have a glass of milk. Yeah. Since you're not eating what, you can have a glass of milk? Since you're not eating what? [? ALEX FLUMENBAUM: ?] Meat. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: There you go. There you go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Since you're not eating meat, so you can have a glass of milk, right? And tell me, did the chicken soup help you? You were sick last week. [? ALEX FLUMENBAUM: ?] I already told you-- yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's why you're-- that's why you here today, right? Because I sent you over chicken soup. [? ALEX FLUMENBAUM: ?] But I am still a little sick. OK. I have the same medicine for you. OK. I'll give you later. [? ALEX FLUMENBAUM: ?] I had medicine this morning. Oh, you had already? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there something she wanted to say? Did Frances want to say one more thing?

words.text: there something she wanted to say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Frances? Is there anything that you didn't say before that you wanted to say now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wish not only my children and grandchildren, the whole world should never have to live through what we went through. I hope the younger generation will do more than we could do to prevent living through something like that. And I also hope that will-- life will continue, that my daughter-in-law, my son, and the grandchildren, they will continue their life the way how they're doing it now. We are very proud and happy. And also, the most of it-- again, I'm saying destiny means a lot probably. We are not that young anymore. But since we are having the children so close by, I feel every day younger. So let's keep it up and let's keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Let me add one thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because one always talks about survivors. And one doesn't, in that word, have so much of a sense of how much that generation contribute-- contributed since they've survived and how much they do today.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you look at my mother and her friends, and you look at the social commitment they have, and the willingness to give time to philanthropic work, that is remarkable to me. Because they look externally rather than have everything focused on them. That, to me, is a tremendous sign of strength. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let me just go back to a wide shot. That's a good place to end.
